explanation: The requirement specifies that when the SelfTest fails (captured by 'selfTestFail'), the controller should not proceed to ventilation (captured by '!ventilating'—i.e., it is never the case that the system is ventilating after a failure). This is a safety property that must be globally enforced, i.e., as long as the SelfTest has failed, ventilation is not permitted at any time. This can be expressed using the release operator: 'selfTestFail V !ventilating', which guarantees that either the SelfTest has not (yet) failed, or, if and when it has failed, ventilation is not enabled at any time afterwards. Previous properties that only require '!ventilating' when 'selfTestFail' is true (e.g., G(selfTestFail -> !ventilating)) may not capture 'proceed' being a temporal transition, or may not globally block all future ventilating once the test fails. The use of the release operator ensures that once a failure occurs, ventilating cannot ever become true.

The natural language specifies that if the SelfTest fails, the controller must not be able to proceed to ventilation. 'SelfTestMode' indicates the system is currently in self-test, and 'selfTestFail' indicates the self-test failure. 'ventilating' indicates that ventilation is started. Thus, the LTL property must state: it is never possible that ventilation starts after a self-test failure while still in SelfTestMode. This is best captured by globally, whenever SelfTestMode is active and selfTestFail becomes true, then from that point onward, ventilating can never become true while still in SelfTestMode. This is captured by the LTL: G((SelfTestMode & selfTestFail) -> G(SelfTestMode -> !ventilating)). Previous incorrect translations could allow ventilating after selfTestFail exits SelfTestMode (not correct here), or might not tie the restriction to the time interval following self-test failure while in SelfTestMode.

The requirement states that if the SelfTest fails, the controller must not be able to proceed to ventilation. In terms of the given atomic propositions, 'selfTestFail' indicates that the self-test has failed, and 'ventilating' indicates that the system is ventilating. To specify that the system must never reach ventilating after a self-test failure, we use the release (R) operator: 'G(selfTestFail -> G(!ventilating))'. This states that globally, if a selfTestFail occurs, from that point forward, ventilating must never be true. This LTL property perfectly captures the natural language as it ensures ventilating is always prevented after a self-test fail. If we tried using only '!ventilating U !selfTestFail', that would not prevent ventilation after failure, so this formulation is required.

The requirement states that, if the SelfTest fails, the controller should not be able to proceed to ventilation. The atomic proposition 'selfTestFail' represents the failure of the SelfTest, and 'ventilating' represents when the controller is ventilating (i.e., ventilation is active). In LTL, to capture the idea that if SelfTestMode is active and a selfTestFail occurs, 'ventilating' must never become true. This can be stated as: 'Globally, if SelfTestMode and selfTestFail both hold, then it is always the case that ventilating will not be true at any point in the future.' Formally, this is captured as G((SelfTestMode & selfTestFail) -> G(!ventilating)). This property prevents the start of ventilation if the self-test fails during SelfTestMode. No previous property is provided for comparison. This LTL captures the intent of the requirement for the SelfTest gating ventilation.

The requirement states: If the SelfTest fails, the controller shall not be able to proceed to ventilation. The most relevant atomic propositions are 'selfTestFail' and 'ventilating'. The property should enforce that whenever the self test fails, the system never reaches ventilation. In LTL, this can be formalized by stating: It is never the case that selfTestFail occurs and then eventually ventilating holds. This can be written as G(selfTestFail -> G(!ventilating)), which ensures that from the point of a self test failure onward, 'ventilating' is never true. This property precisely captures the requirement that a failure of the self-test disables the possibility to proceed to ventilation. Other possible naive translations might just forbid the immediate transition, but this LTL captures that ventilation is forbidden after any self-test failure, globally throughout the system's execution.

The natural language requirement states that when SelfTest fails, the controller must not proceed to ventilation. Using the atomic proposition 'selfTestFail' to indicate the self test failure, and 'ventilating' to indicate that ventilation is ongoing, the LTL property should ensure that at any point if 'selfTestFail' becomes true, then 'ventilating' must never become true afterwards. This is best captured using the 'release' operator: 'selfTestFail V !ventilating', which informally means: either selfTestFail never occurs and nothing is enforced, or, once selfTestFail becomes true, 'ventilating' must remain false forever after. This formula is preferable (compared to, for example, '!selfTestFail U !ventilating'), because it explicitly encodes that it is not permitted to be in the 'ventilating' state when or after 'selfTestFail' holds, covering both the immediate and future restriction on ventilation after the self test fails.

The specification states: 'If the SelfTest fails, the controller shall not be able to proceed to ventilation.' To capture this in LTL using the available atomic propositions, we recognize that 'not able to proceed to ventilation' is best represented by the absence of the 'ventilating' state. Therefore, when 'selfTestFail' occurs (i.e., at any point where selfTestFail is true), it must always be the case that 'ventilating' does not occur afterwards. In LTL, this is expressed as: 'Globally, if selfTestFail holds, then from that point into the future, globally not ventilating holds'—that is, once selfTestFail, then never ventilating (i.e., ventilating is prohibited forever after a self-test failure). This is precisely captured by the formula G(selfTestFail -> G(!ventilating)). This formula is stronger and more accurate than weaker forms like G(selfTestFail -> !ventilating), which would only prohibit ventilating at the instant of selfTestFail, rather than forever after. It rigorously eliminates all paths where, after a self-test failure, ventilation could occur.

The requirement is: If the SelfTest fails, then the controller must not proceed to ventilation. The atomic propositions relevant here are 'selfTestFail' (SelfTest fails), 'SelfTestMode' (system is running SelfTest), and 'ventilating' (ventilation is occurring). We need to guarantee that if 'selfTestFail' holds, then 'ventilating' will never be true (i.e., the system never proceeds to ventilation). In LTL, this can be expressed as: Globally, if 'selfTestFail' holds, then from that point on, 'ventilating' is always false (i.e., ventilating never occurs). This property captures the intent that after a self-test fails, it is never possible to ventilate (no temporal path allows 'ventilating' after 'selfTestFail'). Note that some weaker formulations (e.g., G(selfTestFail -> !ventilating)) are insufficient, as they relate only to points where 'selfTestFail' is true, but a failure could persist after the first occurrence; so we want to permanently inhibit ventilation once 'selfTestFail' occurs. Thus, the best way is to state: "Once selfTestFail occurs, globally thereafter not ventilating." This is expressed as G(selfTestFail -> G(!ventilating)).

The input specifies that if the SelfTest fails, then the controller must not proceed to ventilation. In LTL with the given atomic propositions, 'selfTestFail' indicates the SelfTest has failed, and 'ventilating' indicates the controller is ventilating (i.e., has proceeded to ventilation). The property should ensure that, globally, once a self test failure occurs, ventilation does not happen afterwards, capturing the idea of 'shall not be able to proceed to ventilation.' Thus, the property: G(selfTestFail -> G(!ventilating)) means that whenever 'selfTestFail' becomes true, from that point onwards it is always the case that 'ventilating' is never true. This is a standard safety property pattern to enforce that a system (the ventilator) is not in an undesired state (ventilating) after a detected failure (selfTestFail). There were no previous LTL properties listed, so there is no need to compare with other candidates.

The requirement 'If the SelfTest fails, the controller shall not be able to proceed to ventilation.' means that whenever a self-test fails ('selfTestFail' becomes true), then the system must never enter the ventilation state ('ventilating') at any point in the future. In LTL, this is typically encoded as 'Globally, if selfTestFail holds, then globally in the future, ventilating must not hold'. That is, once selfTestFail is true, ventilating is always false from that point on. Written formally: G(selfTestFail -> G(!ventilating)). This best captures the intent, ensuring no ventilation can proceed after a self-test failure.
